coredata.dc:title: Numerical and experimental analysis of mechanically induced failure in electric vehicle battery modules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mallarapu, Anudeep
Park, Sang-Youn
Lim, Jaeyoung
Han, Seong Bin
Lee, Sang Min
Choi, Byoung-Ho
Han, Yongha
Sunderlin, Nathaniel
Santhanagopalan, Shriram
coredata.dc:description: Mitigating thermal runaway and cell-to-cell propagation is essential for improving the safety of electric and hybrid vehicles. Enhancing digital twin capabilities to predict battery mechanical abuse is particularly critical for automotive and aerospace applications, where crashworthiness is a key concern. Understanding failure conditions and propagation in battery modules during mechanical abuse is complex due to interactions between structural deformation, heat transfer, electrochemical processes, exothermic reactions and mechanical fracture. While prior studies have focused on modeling cell-level behavior, extending these models to module or pack level is necessary for a system level understating of electric vehicle safety. This study develops coupled large deformation finite element models that simultaneously solve for electrochemistry, material failure, internal short circuit and thermal runaway propagation. The models account for mechanical and thermal interactions between lithium-ion cells and other battery components while the contact interfaces are evolving with time. Model-predicted voltage, temperature and force responses are compared with experimental data for validation. The results demonstrate that the approach captures key failure mechanisms, including thermal propagation through heat transfer, electrical propagation from short circuits in parallel-connected cells, and mechanical propagation via penetration and crack formation. These findings show that computational models are valuable tools for understanding battery module failure and providing insight that can reduce the need for extensive experimental testing.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: National Renewable Energy Laboratory: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic description of module-level multiphysics model for battery mechanical abuse simulations (b) Flow chart for calculating current and heat sources from internal short circuit and electrochemical model.
Arrangement of cells within battery module along with thermal and electrical connectivity.
Finite element model case setup for module level mechanical abuse scenarios (a) Out-of-plane Nail Penetration (b) In-plane Hemispherical Indentation.
Mechanical results for nail penetration condition (a) Force vs displacement (b) Stress contours on module structure and cells (cross-section) at displacement of 50 mm.
Nail penetration electrical-thermal results (a) Positive and negative electrode current density at 50s and 600s (b) Temperature distribution within the cells at 50s, 150s, 300s and 600s.
Evolution of electrical and thermal response (a) module voltage results (b) temperature results (model: 5e-9 Ohm-m3 short resistance) (c) location of thermocouples for model predictions and testing.
Mechanical results for hemispherical indentation condition(a) Force vs displacement (b) Stress contours on module structure and cells (cross-section) at displacement of 30 mm.
Model predicted temperature contour results (top-view) for hemispherical indentation condition at various times.
Evolution of electrical and thermal response (a) module voltage results (b) temperature results (c) location of thermocouples for model predictions and testing.

(a) Damaged module post-test (b) Cell-6 after module disassembly (c) Damage patterns near indentation on electrodes after cell disassembly (d) Deformed model with displacement contours (top-view) (e) Temperature contours on Cell-6 during thermal runaway propagation (f) Deformed model of Cell-6 with contours of compressive strain.
Parameters of test articles used in this study.

head.ce:title: Numerical and experimental analysis of mechanically induced failure in electric vehicle battery modules

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitigating thermal runaway and cell-to-cell propagation is essential for improving the safety of electric and hybrid vehicles. Enhancing digital twin capabilities to predict battery mechanical abuse is particularly critical for automotive and aerospace applications, where crashworthiness is a key concern. Understanding failure conditions and propagation in battery modules during mechanical abuse is complex due to interactions between structural deformation, heat transfer, electrochemical processes, exothermic reactions and mechanical fracture. While prior studies have focused on modeling cell-level behavior, extending these models to module or pack level is necessary for a system level understating of electric vehicle safety. This study develops coupled large deformation finite element models that simultaneously solve for electrochemistry, material failure, internal short circuit and thermal runaway propagation. The models account for mechanical and thermal interactions between lithium-ion cells and other battery components while the contact interfaces are evolving with time. Model-predicted voltage, temperature and force responses are compared with experimental data for validation. The results demonstrate that the approach captures key failure mechanisms, including thermal propagation through heat transfer, electrical propagation from short circuits in parallel-connected cells, and mechanical propagation via penetration and crack formation. These findings show that computational models are valuable tools for understanding battery module failure and providing insight that can reduce the need for extensive experimental testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With improvements to lithium-ion battery technology, energy densities and power densities for electric vehicle battery packs have been steadily increasing. While solving several problems that exist with conventional vehicles, electric and hybrid vehicles pose a unique set of concerns when it comes to safety due to the possibility of short-circuiting and exothermic abuse reactions which lead to the release of large amounts of energy in a short duration of time. The rapid failure of batteries, where temperature increases leading to further heat generation due to exothermic reactions, is termed thermal runaway (TR). While thermal runaway can be triggered by internal defects, thermal abuse, or overcharge, mechanical abuse is especially crucial in automotive applications due to crash being one of the major failure modes in vehicles [1]. These events can result in catastrophic failure with the possibility of toxic flammable gas release leading to long-lasting fires that can be challenging to extinguish and the possibility of explosion hazard [2]. While detailed studies have been published characterizing mechanical abuse of batteries at the cell level [3,4], real-world failures of energy storage systems seldom happen in isolated cells. System-level research on battery modules and packs is crucial for improving safety across a wide range of possible failure conditions and for the characterization of electric vehicle crashworthiness.

Batteries are layered composites made of porous electrodes and separators that have highly anisotropic properties resulting in complex mechanisms for mechanical deformation and fracture [5]. Mechanical impact on battery packs can induce internal short circuits (ISC), through failure of the separator leading to contact between anode and cathode layers, or external short circuits which are caused by electrical contact between positive and negative terminals through a foreign body [6]. There are numerous studies available on experimental investigations as well as modeling approaches for mechanical abuse of lithium-ion batteries [7,8]. For high-capacity battery modules, understanding internal phenomena during deformation is crucial to understanding the effect of design parameters and suggesting countermeasures for improving safety. Failure mechanisms can vary between quasistatic and dynamic conditions for mechanical abuse scenarios [9]. Zhu et al. have performed X-ray computed tomography (XCT) experiments revealing that cracks along shear band boundaries lead to ISC during mechanical indentation [3]. Cell level tests provide insights into TR initiation conditions, which are important because it is known that failure mechanisms can determine heat generation rates during thermal runaway and consequently the likelihood of cell-to-cell propagation [10]. Modern EV battery packs have hundreds or thousands of individual cells which means that mitigating the propagation of failure is extremely critical to avoid a catastrophic event. Kim et al. have conducted a series of experimental and numerical studies to understand the role of cooling plates in thermal propagation [11]. Yang et al. have shown that a re-engineered polyurethane foam with fire retardant additives can delay propagation times during cascading thermal runaway [12]. A safety-reinforced layer between the cathode and current collector was examined by Song et al., which can potentially reduce thermal runaway likelihood during mechanical abuse in battery packs [13].

Battery models have been widely used by researchers for analyzing performance [14], degradation [15,16], diagnostics, and safety. Wang et al. has developed several innovative modeling strategies using enhanced Kalman filtering methods to accurately predict the battery state of charge and state of power [17–19]. For safety, studying and testing new mitigation strategies is extremely valuable to improving safety but performing destructive tests on battery modules is very expensive and requires careful control to minimize experimental variability. Therefore, abuse models are especially important to improve the safety of high-capacity battery modules and packs. Several modeling strategies have been developed to address various battery safety concerns. The first aspect of understanding mechanical abuse is modeling the deformation and fracture of the batteries to predict the force and displacement thresholds for triggering internal short circuits (ISC). Mechanical models range from homogenized continuum to representative sandwich models and detailed layer-by-layer models [20–23]. Obtaining accurate constitutive stress-strain relationships and fracture conditions for battery materials is important to effectively predict ISC initiation [24]. Modeling the electrochemical behavior of cells is an important part of understanding heat generation rates from ISC. The literature available on electrochemical models for lithium-ion batteries is huge, with simple equivalent circuit models, detailed porous electrode transport models, empirical models, and single particle models [25–27]. Zhao et al. have used a 3D electrochemical-thermal coupled model to demonstrate the effect of internal-short-circuit resistance and the number of shorted layers on the thermal behavior of the cell [28]. Kim et al. have further investigated the development of non-uniform transport properties and the effect of diffusion limitations on available energy for heat generation during internal short using a detailed electrochemical-thermal model [29]. Multiple approaches have been used for modeling battery thermal runaway ranging from reduced order models to physics-based reaction kinetics models [30–33]. Detailed models have been developed to study the effect of cell venting and vaporization [34,35] as well as gas-phase reactions [36]. Wang et al. introduced a coupled framework with thermal, electrical, mechanical, and fluid dynamic models while suggesting paths to integrating AI with multiphysical modeling [37].

Scaling up models from cell level to module level requires additional considerations like accounting for modeling of structural and thermal components. The added complexity arising from interacting components such as busbars, cells, and module housing creates a need to accurately model interfaces between these parts. Researchers have modeled cell-to-cell propagation by ignoring mechanical deformation in a fixed geometry through thermal conduction and convective heat loss consideration [38]. Although these models predominantly consider conduction, recent developments in computational fluid dynamics models can be useful in understanding heat transfer through airflow in battery packs [39]. Under abuse conditions, module and pack-level models have been largely limited to solving mechanical deformation [40,41]. For failure from mechanical abuse, the large amount of possible loading conditions has led researchers to use machine learning and data-driven methods in combination with physics-based models [42,43]. Improved digital-twin prediction capability needs to be combined with machine learning approaches to simultaneously optimize battery design for a wide range of conditions.

Simultaneously solving multiple processes produces several sources of non-linearities and increased computational cost and development times. Mechanically coupled electrochemical-thermal models have been under development for the past several years. About a decade ago, initial coupled models for mechanical abuse by Zhang et al. using a representative sandwich model were successful in understanding cell behavior during internal short-circuit conditions [44]. Later, Ford Motor Company in collaboration with Livermore Software Technology Corporation developed a powerful coupled simulation framework that is highly scalable and robust for modeling battery cell impact [45]. This LS-DYNA-based framework was adopted by Yuan et al. to accurately predict voltage, force, and temperature during various cell-level mechanical abuse scenarios [46]. In recent years, several studies have used mechanics-coupled multiphysics models to analyze the safety response of lithium-ion batteries at cell-level [47–52]. These studies are quite valuable in understanding the relationship between internal short circuits and thermal runaway initiation as well as to guide cell design targeting safety.

However, to the knowledge of the authors, there are no modeling studies in the literature that analyze mechanical, electrical, and thermal behavior at module or pack level in detail. Previous studies are either limited to cell-level comparison with experiments or simplified physics such as equivalent circuit models without experimental validation. At the module level, the interaction between multiple cell, and structural components along with sliding interfaces adds many model parameters making the calibration and validation process difficult. In this study, we use a coupled mechanical-electrochemical-thermal model of a battery module including its structural and thermal components to analyze safety response under mechanical crush. For this study, low velocity (∼10 mm/min) quasistatic tests for 2 testing conditions were performed at the module-level while monitoring force, voltage, and temperatures. The modeling approach we use is highly scalable and flexible in terms of each submodel as explained in Section 2 of this paper. A temperature-dependent electrochemical model is used, and heat generation rates are accurately calculated from internal-short-circuit, electrochemical discharge, and exothermic abuse reactions. The thermal interface between various geometric domains is activated depending on the contact separation distance. In Section 3, the experimental setup for each abuse loading condition is discussed. We then analyze these experiments along with modeling results in Section 4, providing insights into failure propagation which can be used to design safer and more abuse-tolerant battery modules.

A 3D Multiphysics model has been created using the commercial code LS-DYNA which is a widely used general purpose finite element tool for analyzing large deformation during static and dynamic response of structures [53]. The numerical model used for simulation consists of various sub-models that solve corresponding physical processes. Each cell is modeled as a homogenized continuum using solid hexahedral elements. For mechanics, an Isotropic Crushable Foam model (MAT_063 on LS-DYNA) is used for the cells [54]. This model has been used by previous studies for modeling battery deformation and can accurately predict compression behavior [55]. The modeling approach allows for switching between physics-based models like single particle model or pseudo-two-dimensional model as well as simplified equivalent circuit models which are more empirical and easier to calibrate. For the purpose of this study, a single particle electrochemical model was selected and implemented using user-defined subroutines within LS-DYNA's BatMac framework [45]. Implementation details regarding coupling with LS-DYNA EM solver and comparison of electrical-thermal responses between electrochemical models have been discussed in a previous paper [56]. A two-equation exothermic chemical reaction model is calibrated based on cell-level ARC test data and implemented as a heat source within the thermal solver. Heat conduction is solved within each solid domain while convection with respect to ambient is applied at all outside boundaries. Fig. 1 a illustrates the Multiphysics coupling between the various sub-models being solved simultaneously for prediction of battery failure. Each cell has mechanical and thermal contact interactions with other cells and additional components, which are activated based on distance between boundary surfaces moving with deformation. More details regarding each sub-model (mechanical, thermal, electrochemical, kinetic-abuse) along with model parameters used here are included in a previous paper discussing the cell-level model [57]. Parameters of each sub-model are taken from the previous study and are hence not repeated here.

In this work two distinct internal short circuit mechanisms are considered, namely mechanical and thermally induced internal short circuit. The criteria for short circuit conditions are illustrated in Fig. 1b as a flow chart. Mechanically induced short circuit resistance is applied to nodes which are shared by elements experiencing material failure, i.e. meeting fracture criterion. Apart from short-circuit from mechanical fracture, thermally induced short circuits are applied to nodes reaching temperature greater than separator melting point, which is required to capture module voltage response during thermal runaway propagation. At module-level, there are many interacting components which makes model development especially challenging. Module structural components are modeled with shell and solid elements using Piecewise Linear Isotropic Plasticity model (MAT_024). Mechanical contact is modeled with a static friction coefficient of 0.12 and dynamic friction coefficient of 0.1 across all surfaces using soft constraint penalty formulation. Thermal contact is established for surfaces with separation less than 0.1 mm with a heat transfer coefficient of 1 kW/m2K using THERMAL option in ∗CONTACT keyword on LS-DYNA. The model geometry is shown in Fig. 1 along with loading conditions and is resting on an analytical rigid surface with friction. Indenter (nail/hemisphere) is modeled as a rigid body with shell elements and applied a constant velocity using BOUNDARY_PRESCRIBED_MOTION_RIGID option. Time scaling is applied with a scaling factor of 1000 to the electrochemical-thermal models to enable explicit simulation of mechanics for long time duration.

The electrical and thermal connectivity of cells is depicted in Fig. 2 where the cells are connected in a 2P6S configuration within the module. The electrical connections for the cells within the module are made virtually using EM_ISOPOTENTIAL_CONNECT option on LS-DYNA. Each pair of cells connected in parallel is separated with a thermal insulation pad made of a polyurethane (PU) foam material. For the purpose of this study, cells are numbered from 1 to 12 from top to bottom as shown in the figure. There is direct contact between adjected cells that are not electrically connected to each other (such as Cell-2 to Cell-3 and Cell-4 to Cell-5). The thermal pad has a low thermal conductivity of 0.017 W/mK and is modeled as an isotropic thermal material. The homogenized lithium-ion cells are modeled using anisotropic thermal properties with in-plane thermal conductivity of 27 W/mK and out-of-plane thermal conductivity of 5 W/mK. The negative terminals of Cell-11 and Cell-12 are applied a zero voltage.

This study investigates battery modules composed of 12 high-capacity pouch cells, each with a nominal capacity of 60 Ah. These modules are tested under different mechanical abuse conditions to understand internal failure mechanisms and propagation in electric vehicle battery systems. In the context of vehicle safety, standard testing procedures involve various crash scenarios, such as full-frontal crashes, side pole crashes, and impact from deformable barriers. However, slow deformation tests, despite being less representative of high-speed automotive crashes, are critical for understanding the progression of failure events in a controlled manner. Additionally, such quasi-static tests are useful for initial model calibration, providing a foundation to incorporate rate-dependent material properties in future simulations. Two mechanical abuse experiments were performed to trigger internal-short-circuits within the battery module. The loading conditions considered for this study are described below and shown in Fig. 3 .

Out-of-plane Blunt Nail Penetration: The module is positioned with cell plane oriented parallel to the ground. A 6 mm diameter rod is pushed downwards into the module with a constant velocity of 10 mm/min. A low initial state of charge of 10 % is chosen to avoid triggering thermal runaway and focus on the electrochemical and thermal response within the module. While thermal runaway is the major safety concern in batteries, experiments without thermal runaway are crucial for giving us insights into conditions leading up to thermal runaway and to understand heat generation from internal short circuits.

In-plane Hemispherical Indentation: The module is positioned with cell plane-oriented perpendicular to the ground and longer edge of the cell in horizontal direction. A 20 mm diameter hemispherical indenter moves downward into the module at a constant velocity of 10 mm/min. This test is conducted with a 50 % initial SoC to study thermal runaway propagation. Under this condition, only two central cells experience direct mechanical abuse, while the rest of the cells are subjected to indirect thermal and electrical interactions. This scenario simulates the onset of thermal runaway within the central cells triggering a chain reaction that could propagate through the entire module.

To ensure consistency during testing, the battery module is firmly held in place using a fixture that prevents movement. The parameters of the test articles are shown in Table 1 while the loading conditions are shown in Table 2 . By testing the battery modules under these conditions, the study aims to capture the various failure modes during mechanical abuse. Total reaction force experienced by the indenter is measured using a load cell connected on top of the indenter. Total module voltage is measured across the positive and negative terminal while thermocouples are placed in multiple locations along the module as later shown in Figs. 6 and 9.

The mechanical, thermal and electrical repose under the two mechanical abuse scenarios is presented for models and experiments. The results are compared to capture key failure mechanisms and internal states during abuse.

This section discusses the results of blunt rod penetration loading condition. Under this loading condition, thermal propagation is insignificant because of the low initial state of charge and consequently low heat generation. Here, the failure propagation is primarily through mechanical fracture of a cell providing pathway for the intruding object to fracture a subsequent cell. Each cell experiences an internal short circuit, but the heat generated is not sufficient under these heat transfer conditions to raise the cell temperature to initiate thermal runaway. This allows us to study voltage and temperature changes in the absence of degradation reactions occurring at high temperatures. We first look at mechanical results followed by electrical and thermal results.

Fig. 4 a shows the comparison of force vs displacement results for the model and experiment. Initial slope of the force-displacement curve from the model is stiffer than experimentally measured response. Model results from 2 values of fracture criterion (0.55 and 0.6) are shown in the figure. The peak force obtained from the experiment is
∼
3.3 kN whereas the models predict peak forces of
∼
2.4–2.7 kN. Force drop corresponds to elements deletion upon meeting fracture criterion. Mechanical model uses an explicit solver and can work for highly deformed elements. But, under extreme distortions, the thermal solver will have problems in obtaining a converged solution. Even though peak force is better predicted by
ε f
= 0.6 within the model, this case leads to convergence issues within thermal solver due to negative volumes momentarily caused near the fractured location. Therefore, the following discussion on electrical and thermal results uses mechanical model with
ε f
= 0.55. Stress contours on module structure and individual cells are shown in Fig. 4b.

There are several sources of error between model predictions and experimental results for the mechanical response. From our previous work, mechanical results match much better at cell level. Increased complexity and additional components at module level leads to greater error in force and deformation. Material properties of structural components need to be properly characterized. Using a homogenized mesh simplifies the model and calibration process but affects accuracy of the models. The noisiness in model prediction after fracture arises from element deletions occurring at different times as indenter penetrates through the cells. Also in reality, high temperatures affect mechanical properties significantly. Hence incorporating thermal dependance of material properties will improve force prediction beyond initial short circuit.

Three-dimensional distribution of electrical and thermal results from the model can be seen in Fig. 5
. The model uses a mechanical fracture based internal short circuit criterion as previously described. As seen in Fig. 5a, when cell-1 fractures from excessive strain, an internal short circuit develops near the damaged region leading to temperature rise (Fig. 5b). Current in positive current collectors (results on left) flows towards the short leading to rapid discharge within the cell. Simultaneously, current flows away from the short within the negative current collector (results on right). At this point (time = 50 s) Cell-2 is mechanically undamaged but being electrically connected in parallel to Cell-1 causes it to discharge through the tabs from an external short circuit. At a higher state of charge, Cell-2 would simultaneously experience a combination of severe electrical abuse (as an external short circuit) as well as thermal abuse (through heat transfer from Cell-1) along with mechanical short circuiting. In this case, by the time Cell-2 gets mechanically fractured (time = 100 s, Fig. 5b) there is not enough remaining capacity within the cell to cause significant heat generation from the internal short circuit. As a result, heat generation is only observed for every alternate cell that is mechanically fractured. At 600s (Fig. 5a) multiple cells can be seen discharging simultaneously from internal short circuits. As expected, the current density is progressively higher for more recently shorted cells due to greater residual capacity (and hence higher cell voltage).

Fig. 6 shows the comparison of electrical and thermal response from the model prediction to experimental measurements. Multiple values of internal short circuit resistance (Rs) were used to observe voltage response of the module. These voltage results are shown in Fig. 6a, where it can be seen that high resistance short (
R s
= 25 ·
10
− 9
Ω
m 3
)
causes very slow discharge while low resistance short (
R s
= 1 ·
10
− 9
Ω
m 3
)
causes quick discharge of each cell within few seconds of fracture. Note that these cells are already at a low state of charge (10 %) and hence there is not much capacity left in the cell. Temperature results from
5 ·
10
− 9
Ω
m 3
short resistance simulation, are compared with experimental results in Fig. 6b and the corresponding thermocouple locations are shown in Fig. 6c.

For the In-Plane hemispherical indentation case, the mechanical results are shown in Fig. 7 . In this scenario, the cells beneath the indenter experience load along the orientation of electrode layers, which can lead to internal bulking of these layers. Forces are significantly underpredicted by the model which is a result of using homogenized model approximation for a layered composite structure. During the experiment, the force on indenter reaches approximately 2.9 kN at around 30 mm displacement while model predicts a peak force of only ∼2.0 kN at 25 mm displacement. The stress contours reveal that most of the load is borne by the two central cells (Cell-6 and Cell-7) which are also the only cells to experience fracture (element deletion). Mechanically induced internal short circuit (ISC) is experienced only by these two cells, whereas the remaining cells are shorted from thermally induced short circuits as temperature reaches above the melting point of separator material (assumed to be
170
°C
). Mechanically induced ISC regions are applied a short resistance of
R s
= 10 ·
10
− 9
Ω
m 3
while thermally induced ISC is taken as
R s
= 50 ·
10
− 8
Ω
m 3
.

As seen in Fig. 8
, thermal runaway is triggered in cell-6 and cell-7 almost immediately (within 2–3 s) after mechanical failure criterion is activated. As there is thermal insulation between Cell-5 and Cell-6 as well as cell-7 and cell-8, thermal runaway is contained within the mechanically damaged cells for approximately 50 s before triggering thermal runaway in adjacent cells. After that, each pair of cells, 4–5 and 8–9 experience thermal runaway propagation simultaneously as there is no insulation between them. It can be seen that the PU foam material is effective in slowing down cell-to-cell propagation causing full module propagation to last over 150 s. In the model results some asymmetry arises from cell-6 experiencing internal short circuit first compared to Cell-7. This leads to faster completion of thermal runaway propagation on one side (towards Cell-1) compared to the other side (towards Cell-12). But it should be noted that this result is entirely stochastic as model boundary conditions are symmetric. As reproducibility is low even for cell-level mechanical abuse tests [58], additional factors at module-level can cause higher uncertainty. Such randomness can arise both in experiments as well as simulations because mechanically induced thermal runaway is extremely sensitive to initial and boundary conditions.

Comparison of model voltage and temperature profiles to experimental data is shown in Fig. 9. The thermocouple locations corresponding to Fig. 9b are show in Fig. 9c. The voltage from experiments (Fig. 9a) is seen so drop unrealistically to a low value from ∼160s to ∼190s which is not predicted by the model. This could be due to temporary melting or loss of connection at some location during thermal runaway resulting in misfunctioning of voltage sensing. Both model and experiment predict similar time for module voltage to go to 0V which corresponds to the time all cells go into thermal runaway (Fig. 9b). As seen in Fig. 9a, initially mechanical abuse on Cell-6 and Cell-7 leads voltage to drop to ∼14.2 V as these cells are discharged through the short. At this point, Cell-5 and Cell-8 which are also discharged as they are connected in parallel to the damaged cells. Remaining voltage drops in the model occur due to short circuiting from high temperatures causing separator melting.

From Fig. 9b, thermocouple near the indentation location can be seen to reach beyond
700
°C
owing to combined heat generation form internal short circuit and exothermic decomposition reactions. In experiments each thermocouple records a smaller peak at ∼
350
°C
followed by a sharp temperature rise going into full thermal runaway, while the model predicts only quick rise in temperature all the way till peak temperature. This suggests that the model is unable to capture reaction kinetics well during thermal runaway induced by internal short-circuit. While kinetic abuse parameters were calibrated using ARC test data, it should be noted that heat generation rates during complex abuse conditions may not match with the model prediction.

Fig. 10 (a–c) show images of the test article after the thermal runaway propagation experiment. Corresponding model results of displacement, temperature and strain are shown in Fig. 10(d–f). From top view (Fig. 10a) it is evident that even after all cells have undergone thermal runaway, the battery module is still structurally intact. Mechanical deformation is localized close to indenter as shown in Fig. 10d as other cells and thermal insulation pads experience very low displacement. In Fig. 10b, cell-6 is shown after removal of outer pouch material. For directly damaged cells, it is observed that molten Aluminum from the current collector forms beads at the opposite side of indentation. This confirms that temperature of cells reaches greater than melting temperature of aluminum (
660
°C
) as measured by thermocouple 4 in Fig. 8b. Temperature contours on Cell-6 at 150s are shown in Fig. 9e with thermal runaway originating at short circuit location and propagating through the cell. Fig. 10c shows a close up of indentation location with complex buckling indued failure, however the homogenized model simplifies the mechanical fracture to a strain-based criterion resulting in a damage pattern as shown in Fig. 10f.

This study presents a comprehensive approach that combines coupled mechanical-electrochemical-thermal multiphysics modeling with experimental comparison to evaluate battery safety at electric vehicle (EV) module level. By scaling models physics-based modeling efforts beyond the cell level, this work addresses the complexities introduced by structural elements, thermal insulation and electrical interconnections - factors often not fully captured in traditional battery safety analyses. The key strength of this approach is the use of high-fidelity coupled models to interpret mechanical abuse experiments, which are inherently destructive and offer limited sensing of internal variables. These models allow detailed insights into internal mechanical stresses, heat transfer, and electrical behavior, offering a deeper understanding of failure mechanisms that are difficult to observe directly. This modeling framework supports digital twins for batteries which are virtual, physics-informed replicas that can simulate real-time module behavior. Two slow mechanical deformation scenarios were examined: though-plane blunt nail penetration and in-plane hemispherical indentation. Results show that failure propagation mechanisms differ depending on the loading condition and state of charge, with mechanical and thermal interactions dominating in different cases. While key metrics such as propagation times and peak temperatures were captured well, gaps in voltage and temperature profiles point to the need for enhanced modeling of internal short circuits and decomposition kinetics to improve predictive accuracy.

Anudeep Mallarapu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sang-Youn Park: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Jaeyoung Lim: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Seong Bin Han: Formal analysis. Sang Min Lee: Investigation, Formal analysis. Byoung-Ho Choi: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yongha Han: Supervision, Project administration. Nathaniel Sunderlin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shriram Santhanagopalan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by Hyundai Motor Corporation, Korea and National Renewable Energy Laboratory, USA. The research was supported using computational resources sponsored by the Department of Energy's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, located at the National Renewable Energy Laboratory.
